title: Laos
subtitle: 18 Days · Luang Prabang
body:
Laotian Charm

Laos was arguably the slowest and most laid-back of all the countries I've cycled through thus far. Admittedly, there wasn't much time spent actually riding - only 4 days in the saddle out of the 2.5 weeks I was there.

It was not the time of year to breathe in the Laotian air, let alone engage in cardiovascular activities. It was the dry season… which also means the burn season - a time when everyone burns old crops to make room for the new. Necessary I suppose, but a detriment to one's health.

With so much smog and PM 2.5, the sky was stained orange. Where viewscapes are impossible to enjoy, and the sun can be witnessed with the naked eye… although, I still don't think that's a good idea.

Ironically, I was aware of this season before this tour of Asia and attempted to plan around it, and STILL found myself smack in the middle of the season… Poor planning on my part. I was told it was the worst it's ever been. Very Apocalyptic-like, but oddly mystical in a way.

Poor air be damned, I still attempted to experience all that Laos had to offer.

I left the bike in the old capital, Luang Prabang, and hopped on the newly constructed Chinese rail line to Vang Vieng - a place known for its outdoor recreation, caves, lagoon, waterfalls, etc.

Then there's the weight underneath it all. Laos is one of the most heavily bombed countries - with evidence of old bomb shells thrown about like decoration.

Today, Laos is one of the poorest countries in Southeast Asia and you feel the disparity. I was there on a journey of "self actualization," while many of the people are doing their best to simply survive. I'm on the top of Maslow's Hierarchy of needs and many in Laos exist closer to the bottom… you feel that difference. There's a form of guilt that comes with it.

On top of it all, geopolitics continues to shape the lives of the Laotian people. Ostensibly, the Chinese are providing infrastructure with loans that are impossible to repay - a means for cheap resource extraction I suppose. The convoys of Chinese trucks grinding the already shattered roads to dust. And a petrol shortage caused by the war in Iran. Many of the stations I passed were closed due to an absence of petrol.

Even amongst all the difficulties, the Laotians still find ways to celebrate.

Pi Mai Lao — Laotian New Year — erupted in the streets while I was there. Amongst various celebrations is a three day water fight… one of the primary reasons I stuck around for so long. Luang Prabang being one of the best places to experience it… people from all over the country flock to the old capital to drink BeerLao and get soaked.

Laos is a charming country, with a laid back energy. It sorta feels like beach town, but without the beach… I'll be back when there's fresher air.
